title: Treating mycoplasma in a laying flock
subtitle: Managing chronic respiratory disease without eliminating your birds.
excerpt: Mycoplasma gallisepticum is present in most backyard laying flocks and rarely causes problems until something stresses the birds. When it flares, the symptoms look like a bad cold that won't fully clear. Here's how to spot an active episode, what antibiotic protocol is available, and how to manage the flock to reduce recurrence.
sourceNotes: DEFRA/APHA poultry disease guidance. APHA Animal and Plant Health Agency, Mycoplasma infection in poultry factsheet. University of Wisconsin Extension poultry health guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: A group of small bacteria without cell walls; Mycoplasma gallisepticum causes chronic respiratory disease in poultry and is common in backyard flocks.
The wet, clicking or bubbling sound heard in the throat of a hen with active respiratory infection, caused by mucus in the airway.

content.text: in a laying flock rarely means losing the flock. It means managing a low-level infection that flares when the birds are under stress: a new bird added without quarantine, a bad moult, a damp autumn, a sudden diet change. The goal is not to clear it entirely, which rarely happens in a mixed-age flock. The goal is suppression: keeping the infection quiet enough that the birds are comfortable and laying.
Nasal discharge, watery to foamy, often more visible in one nostril
audible when you hold the bird close
Swollen infraorbital sinuses, the area directly below the eye looks puffy
Egg production drops, sometimes sharply, in affected birds

Mycoplasma requires a prescription antibiotic. Tylosin and enrofloxacin are the ones most commonly used. Your vet will prescribe based on their assessment. Follow the withdrawal period on the label: it varies by product, and you should not sell eggs during the course.

Don't medicate without veterinary guidance. Antibiotic resistance in Mycoplasma is a real problem. The wrong antibiotic, or the right one at the wrong dose, makes the infection harder to treat next time.
Improve ventilation. Mycoplasma thrives in damp, still air. Check the coop for blocked vents and increase airflow at roof height.
Reduce stress triggers. Mixed-age introductions, overcrowding, sudden diet changes, and predator disturbance all suppress immunity. During a flare, lock down unnecessary changes.
Quarantine new birds for four weeks before adding them to the flock. Introduce birds from MG-clean tested flocks where possible.

Mycoplasma is transmissible through the egg, an infected hen can pass the infection to her chicks in ovo. If you want to hatch eggs from an affected flock into a clean pen, discuss with your vet whether a dipping or antibiotic treatment protocol is appropriate before incubating. Most small flocks don't try to produce MG-clean chicks; they simply manage the chronic infection in the adults.
